events.text: Sam found a shivering puppy alone in the rain.
The puppy had no collar and looked very hungry.
Sam took the puppy home and gave it food and a warm blanket.
He made posters and asked everyone in the street about the owner.
A girl recognised the puppy and hugged her lost pet tightly.
Sam felt proud that he had brought them back together.
The bakery sold out of doughnuts by lunchtime.
Mia and Tom decided to build the biggest sandcastle on the beach.
They collected buckets, spades and a flag to put on top.
They dug a deep moat and packed the towers tall and firm.
A big wave splashed close and they raced to build a wall.
By sunset the castle stood proudly with its flag flapping.
Everyone passing stopped to admire what they had made.
Dad watched the football scores on his phone.
Priya was chosen to play the lead in the school play.
She felt nervous because she had so many lines to learn.
Every evening she practised her lines with her mum.
On the day, she forgot one line but quickly carried on.
The audience clapped loudly when the play ended.
Priya beamed, glad she had been so brave.
The cat next door was asleep on the wall.
Ben woke up to find the whole garden covered in thick snow.
School was closed, so he pulled on his boots and coat.
He and his sister built a snowman with a carrot nose.
They had a friendly snowball fight until their gloves were soaked.
Warm and cosy indoors, they drank hot chocolate by the window.
They agreed it had been the best day of the winter.
The postman delivered a parcel to number twelve.

activities.title: The Robot at the Science Fair

events.text: Leo had spent weeks building a small robot for the science fair.
On the morning of the fair, the robot suddenly refused to switch on.
Hiding his panic, Leo opened the casing and checked every wire.
He discovered a loose battery connection and carefully reattached it.
When the judges arrived, the robot whirred to life and rolled forward.
Leo won second prize, relieved that his hard work had paid off.
The canteen was serving pizza that lunchtime.
His neighbour was repainting their front fence.
While walking the dog, Amelia heard a frightened cry from the riverbank.
A small child's football had drifted out and he was reaching for it.
Thinking quickly, Amelia told him to stay back from the slippery edge.
She found a long branch and stretched it out across the water.
The boy gripped the branch and Amelia pulled him safely to dry ground.
His grateful mother thanked her again and again for her quick thinking.
The ice cream van played its tune in the car park.
A jogger checked the time on her watch.
Old Mr Finch had kept the lighthouse burning every night for forty years.
One stormy evening, the great lamp flickered and went dark.
Knowing ships were near, he climbed the winding stairs against the wind.
He worked by torchlight to replace the broken part with trembling hands.
At last the beam blazed out again, sweeping across the wild sea.
By dawn, every ship had passed the rocks safely.
The village shop had a sale on woollen hats.
A seagull landed on the harbour railing.
Digging in the garden, Noah's spade struck something hard and metal.
He uncovered a rusty tin box with a date scratched into the lid.
Carefully, he prised it open to find letters and photographs inside.
The letters had been written by a boy his age fifty years before.
Noah decided to make his own capsule for someone to find one day.
He buried it deep, imagining the future child who would open it.
His sister was practising the piano indoors.
The bins were collected early that Tuesday.
